2022 · TMLR · Benchmarks & Evals

Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

Before HELM you couldn't actually compare two language models — every paper reported accuracy on whatever datasets it picked, under its own prompting. HELM made evaluation a framework: 7 metrics across 16 core scenarios for 30 models under identical conditions, lifting model×scenario coverage from 17.9% to 96.0% and turning "which model is best" into "best at what, and what does it cost you."

Liang, Bommasani, Lee, Tsipras, Soylu, Yasunaga, …, Mai, Y. Zhang, Koreeda — Stanford CRFM
venue TMLR citations 1,861 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06-20
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

Benchmarks orient a field — they encode what counts as progress. By late 2022 the benchmark for language models was a patchwork. Each new model reported on whatever datasets its authors chose, scored on a single metric (almost always accuracy), under whatever prompting they happened to use. Two models could share no evaluation in common, and there was no controlled way to put them head-to-head.

The paper quantifies the mess: across the datasets HELM later designates as core, a typical prominent model had been evaluated on just 17.9% of them — "with some prominent models not sharing a single scenario in common." And accuracy was nearly the only thing anyone measured. Calibration, robustness to typos, fairness across dialects, social bias, toxicity, inference efficiency — the desiderata a deployed system actually has to satisfy — were reported rarely, never side-by-side, and never for the same models on the same tasks. You couldn't see what a point of accuracy was costing you, because nobody was measuring the other axes.

HELM's claim is that holistic evaluation is now a prerequisite, not a luxury: these models were already shipping in commercial APIs and products (the OpenAI playground, GitHub Copilot), so "the absence of an evaluation standard compromises the community's ability to clearly and rigorously understand the overall landscape."

// how it works

The method

HELM is a framework, not a dataset. Its object of evaluation is the language model itself — a general-purpose text-in/text-out interface — rather than any one task-specific system. Three design commitments follow.

1 · Taxonomize, then cover broadly (and name what's missing)

HELM first maps the space of scenarios (a use case = a task × a domain × who is using it, when, where, and in what language) and the space of metrics (the desiderata we want), then deliberately selects a feasible subset and flags the gaps it leaves — e.g. question answering for neglected English dialects, metrics for trustworthiness. The headline instantiation: 16 core scenarios (NaturalQuestions open- and closed-book, BoolQ, NarrativeQA, QuAC, HellaSwag, OpenBookQA, TruthfulQA, MMLU, MS MARCO regular & TREC, XSUM, CNN/DailyMail, IMDB, CivilComments, RAFT) plus 7 targeted evaluations over 26 targeted scenarios (language modeling, knowledge, reasoning, memorization/copyright, disinformation, bias, toxicity) — 42 scenarios in all, 21 of them not previously used in mainstream LM evaluation.

2 · Measure many metrics for every scenario

This is the core move. Rather than one metric per dataset, HELM measures 7 metrics — accuracy, calibration, robustness, fairness, bias, toxicity, efficiency — for each core scenario, "to the extent possible (87.5% of the time)." Robustness and fairness are defined as worst-case accuracy under perturbations (typos and invariances for robustness; dialect and demographic rewrites for fairness); calibration is expected calibration error; toxicity is scored by an automated classifier (PerspectiveAPI) and bias by demographic word-association counts; efficiency is an idealized, hardware-denoised inference runtime. Measuring every desideratum on every scenario is what makes the trade-offs visible.

3 · Standardize the adaptation

To compare language models rather than bespoke systems, the way each model is adapted to a scenario has to be held fixed. HELM adapts all 30 models by the same simple 5-shot prompting — pioneered by GPT-3 — with no fine-tuning, no chain-of-thought, and no per-model prompt optimization. That is what licenses head-to-head comparison: each model's per-metric rank is its win rate against every other model across scenarios.

win-rate(M) = Σs∈S ΣM′≠M 𝟙[ scores(M) > scores(M′) ]|S| · (|models| − 1) // a model's head-to-head win rate on a metric — the fraction of (scenario, opponent) pairs it wins; the basis of the per-metric rankings in Fig. 26

The whole run is open: HELM released every raw prompt and completion, plus a modular toolkit for adding scenarios, models, metrics, and prompting strategies — and framed itself as a living benchmark meant to be continuously re-run as new models appear.

// what they found

The results

The run itself was large: 30 models × 42 scenarios, a total of 12.2 billion tokens and 17.4 million queries, costing $38,001 in commercial API calls plus about 19,500 GPU-hours for the open models — and yielding 25 top-level findings. A few stand out.

Instruction-tuning beat scale

Across the core scenarios, text-davinci-002 was best on accuracy, robustness, and fairness, and the 52B Anthropic-LM v4-s3 sat in the top 3 on all three — despite being more than 10× smaller than TNLG v2 (530B), the second-most-accurate model. Those two were the only instruction-tuned models in the field (beyond small OpenAI variants), so their dominance points squarely at instruction-tuning, not raw parameter count, as the source of broad gains.

The multi-metric lens paid off: trade-offs you couldn't see before

  • Robustness & fairness track accuracy — but not perfectly. Strong correlations across all scenarios, yet the most accurate model isn't always the most robust or fair: on NarrativeQA, TNLG v2 (530B) collapses from 72.6% to 38.9% accuracy once robustness perturbations are applied.
  • Calibration has no consistent relationship with accuracy. It depends on the scenario — on HellaSwag, improving accuracy worsens calibration; on OpenBookQA, it improves it.
  • Generation harms are low and roughly constant across models in the core scenarios — though the authors caution that even low bias/toxicity can do real harm, and that the targeted evals are needed to see the differences.
  • No strong accuracy–efficiency trade-off across the 30 models, though within any one family bigger is more accurate and more expensive, and only a subset of models sit on the accuracy–efficiency Pareto frontier.

Knowledge, reasoning, and an uncomfortable fairness result

On knowledge, text-davinci-002 led TruthfulQA at 62.0% versus 36.2% for the next model. On reasoning, the code models won even on natural-language tasks: code-davinci-002 hit 52.1% on GSM8K, where the next best (text-davinci-002) managed 35.0% and no other model cleared 16%. And on the BBQ bias benchmark, text-davinci-002 was most accurate by a wide margin (89.5%) — but the three most accurate models were precisely the ones whose biases in ambiguous contexts aligned with social discrimination, while every other model leaned the opposite way. Accuracy and social bias pulled in the same direction.

ModelWhat it leadsNumber
text-davinci-002accuracy / robustness / fairness; TruthfulQA62.0%
Anthropic-LM v4-s3 (52B)top-3 on all three, at >10× smaller scale—
code-davinci-002reasoning leader — GSM8K52.1%
TNLG v2 (530B)NarrativeQA accuracy → under perturbation72.6→38.9%
OPT (175B)HellaSwag, separate-0-shot vs joint-5-shot prompt79.1→30.2%

Two structural findings frame the rest. There is a consistent open-vs-closed gap on every core scenario (closed/limited-access models on top), though the 2022 open releases — OPT-175B, BLOOM-176B, GLM-130B — were visibly narrowing it. And scale only half-predicts quality: every model that wins accuracy head-to-heads above 55% is at least 50B parameters, yet within no single scenario does scale rank models across families, and bits-per-byte on The Pile is a poor predictor of downstream accuracy. Finally, just by evaluating everything under unified conditions, HELM surfaced a new state of the art hiding in plain sight: text-davinci-002's 74.4% ROUGE-L on NarrativeQA beat the QA-specialized UnifiedQA-v2 (67.4%).

// does it hold up?

The eval read

HELM's contribution is the methodology, and the methodology has aged extraordinarily well — almost every serious eval since inherits its template. But read as a 2022 result, several things qualify the leaderboard, and to the paper's credit it names most of them itself.

It's a snapshot, and the snapshot is old. HELM v1 is a photograph of late-2022: the strongest model in it is text-davinci-002, and the most capable systems of the era — PaLM, Gopher, Chinchilla — were never evaluated (the authors say they "expect [them] to be quite accurate"). GPT-4, Claude, and the entire chat/reasoning-model generation didn't exist yet. The specific rankings are now historical curiosities; what endures is the protocol, which is exactly why HELM was built as a living benchmark.

Every number is downstream of one adaptation choice. Standardizing on simple 5-shot prompting is what makes comparison fair — but it also means each score is a floor under a particular regime, not a measure of capability. HELM's own ablations show how load-bearing this is: OPT-175B scores 79.1% on HellaSwag with answer-separate 0-shot prompting and 30.2% — near random — with answer-joint 5-shot, the same model and task. No chain-of-thought, no fine-tuning, no prompt optimization. That ceiling is most consequential precisely on reasoning, where code-davinci-002's dominance hints that the trends would shift under CoT.

Contamination is acknowledged, not controlled. The models train on undocumented web-scale corpora; HELM catalogs known contamination evidence in an appendix but concedes the extent "largely remains unclear," and closed models like text-davinci-002 have entirely unknown training data. For any scenario built from public datasets, you can't rule out that part of the leaderboard is memorization — a structural limit, not a fixable bug.

Some metric names outrun their measurements. "Fairness" is operationalized as worst-case accuracy under dialect/typo perturbations; "bias" as demographic word-association counts; "toxicity" via a single automated classifier (PerspectiveAPI) with its own documented demographic skew. These are cheap, reproducible, and a real advance over measuring nothing — but they under-determine the social constructs they're named for, and the finding that core-scenario toxicity is "low and roughly constant" partly reflects a blunt instrument (HELM's own targeted toxicity eval is needed to see the real differences). The single aggregate win-rate, similarly, flattens wildly heterogeneous scenarios — the honest read is the per-scenario, per-metric grid, not the rank.

baselines

30 models spanning open / limited-access / closed — broad for 2022, but the strongest systems of the era (PaLM, Gopher, Chinchilla) are absent and the leader is text-davinci-002. A current snapshot, not a current ranking.

contamination

Cataloged in Appendix G but the extent "largely remains unclear"; closed leaders have undocumented training data, so memorization on public datasets can't be excluded.

what's missed

Capability under CoT / fine-tuning / optimized prompts (one 5-shot regime drives every number — OPT-175B swings 79.1→30.2% on prompt format alone); thin proxies for fairness/bias/toxicity; near-entirely English (Fig. 10).

would raise confidence

Re-running the living benchmark on current chat/reasoning models under multiple adaptation regimes; human-grounded fairness/toxicity metrics; explicit decontamination; reporting the grid over the aggregate.

// bottom line

HELM didn't win by topping a leaderboard — it redefined what a leaderboard is. The 2022 numbers are history, but the move from "report accuracy on the datasets you picked" to "measure seven desiderata across dozens of scenarios under identical prompting, and say out loud what's missing" is the template MT-Bench, LegalBench, HealthBench, and the SWE-bench line all inherited. Its candor about its own confounds — prompt sensitivity, contamination, English-centrism — is a big part of why it aged into infrastructure instead of a stale result.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. HELM made LM evaluation multi-metric and standardized. Seven metrics (accuracy, calibration, robustness, fairness, bias, toxicity, efficiency) across 16 core scenarios for 30 models under one 5-shot protocol — raising model×scenario coverage from 17.9% to 96.0% and exposing trade-offs a single accuracy number hides.
  2. The headline finding was that instruction-tuning, not scale, bought broad gains — text-davinci-002 and a 52B Anthropic model beat a 530B model on accuracy, robustness, and fairness — while the multi-metric lens surfaced the uncomfortable parts: robustness/fairness track accuracy, calibration doesn't, and the most accurate BBQ models were the most socially biased.
  3. The lasting value is the method and its honesty. Every number is a floor under one prompting regime, contamination can't be ruled out, and "fairness/toxicity" are thin proxies — limitations HELM named itself. That candor, plus the living-benchmark design, is why its framework (not its 2022 ranking) became the standard for serious evals.
